This is used by the 'db' cache backend to determine where to start
culling.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.unification_cast_sql
documentation:
Given a field instance, return the SQL that casts the result of a union
to that type. The resulting string should contain a '%s' placeholder
for the expression being cast.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.date_extract_sql
documentation:
Given a lookup_type of 'year', 'month', or 'day', return the SQL that
extracts a value from the given date field field_name.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.date_trunc_sql
documentation:
Given a lookup_type of 'year', 'month', or 'day', return the SQL that
truncates the given date or datetime field field_name to a date object
with only the given specificity.

If `tzname` is provided, the given value is truncated in a specific
timezone.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.datetime_cast_date_sql
documentation: Return the SQL to cast a datetime value to date value.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.datetime_cast_time_sql
documentation: Return the SQL to cast a datetime value to time value.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.datetime_extract_sql
documentation:
Given a lookup_type of 'year', 'month', 'day', 'hour', 'minute', or
'second', return the SQL that extracts a value from the given
datetime field field_name.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.datetime_trunc_sql
documentation:
Given a lookup_type of 'year', 'month', 'day', 'hour', 'minute', or
'second', return the SQL that truncates the given datetime field
field_name to a datetime object with only the given specificity.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.time_trunc_sql
documentation:
Given a lookup_type of 'hour', 'minute' or 'second', return the SQL
that truncates the given time or datetime field field_name to a time
object with only the given specificity.

If `tzname` is provided, the given value is truncated in a specific
timezone.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.time_extract_sql
documentation:
Given a lookup_type of 'hour', 'minute', or 'second', return the SQL
that extracts a value from the given time field field_name.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.deferrable_sql
documentation:
Return the SQL to make a constraint "initially deferred" during a
CREATE TABLE statement.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.distinct_sql
documentation:
Return an SQL DISTINCT clause which removes duplicate rows from the
result set. If any fields are given, only check the given fields for
duplicates.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.fetch_returned_insert_columns
documentation:
Given a cursor object that has just performed an INSERT...RETURNING
statement into a table, return the newly created data.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.field_cast_sql
documentation:
Given a column type (e.g. 'BLOB', 'VARCHAR') and an internal type
(e.g. 'GenericIPAddressField'), return the SQL to cast it before using
it in a WHERE statement. The resulting string should contain a '%s'
placeholder for the column being searched against.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.force_no_ordering
documentation:
Return a list used in the "ORDER BY" clause to force no ordering at
all. Return an empty list to include nothing in the ordering.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.for_update_sql
documentation: Return the FOR UPDATE SQL clause to lock rows for an update operation.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.limit_offset_sql
documentation: Return LIMIT/OFFSET SQL clause.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.last_executed_query
documentation:
Return a string of the query last executed by the given cursor, with
placeholders replaced with actual values.

`sql` is the raw query containing placeholders and `params` is the
sequence of parameters. These are used by default, but this method
exists for database backends to provide a better implementation
according to their own quoting schemes.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.last_insert_id
documentation:
Given a cursor object that has just performed an INSERT statement into
a table that has an auto-incrementing ID, return the newly created ID.

`pk_name` is the name of the primary-key column.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.lookup_cast
documentation:
Return the string to use in a query when performing lookups
("contains", "like", etc.). It should contain a '%s' placeholder for
the column being searched against.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.max_in_list_size
documentation:
Return the maximum number of items that can be passed in a single 'IN'
list condition, or None if the backend does not impose a limit.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.max_name_length
documentation:
Return the maximum length of table and column names, or None if there
is no limit.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.no_limit_value
documentation:
Return the value to use for the LIMIT when we are wanting "LIMIT
infinity". Return None if the limit clause can be omitted in this case.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.pk_default_value
documentation:
Return the value to use during an INSERT statement to specify that
the field should use its default value.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.prepare_sql_script
documentation:
Take an SQL script that may contain multiple lines and return a list
of statements to feed to successive cursor.execute() calls.

Since few databases are able to process raw SQL scripts in a single
cursor.execute() call and PEP 249 doesn't talk about this use case,
the default implementation is conservative.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.process_clob
documentation:
Return the value of a CLOB column, for backends that return a locator
object that requires additional processing.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.return_insert_columns
documentation:
For backends that support returning columns as part of an insert query,
return the SQL and params to append to the INSERT query. The returned
fragment should contain a format string to hold the appropriate column.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.compiler
documentation:
Return the SQLCompiler class corresponding to the given name,
in the namespace corresponding to the `compiler_module` attribute
on this backend.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.quote_name
documentation:
Return a quoted version of the given table, index, or column name. Do
not quote the given name if it's already been quoted.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.regex_lookup
documentation:
Return the string to use in a query when performing regular expression
lookups (using "regex" or "iregex"). It should contain a '%s'
placeholder for the column being searched against.

If the feature is not supported (or part of it is not supported), raise
NotImplementedError.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.savepoint_create_sql
documentation:
Return the SQL for starting a new savepoint. Only required if the
"uses_savepoints" feature is True. The "sid" parameter is a string
for the savepoint id.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.savepoint_commit_sql
documentation: Return the SQL for committing the given savepoint.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.savepoint_rollback_sql
documentation: Return the SQL for rolling back the given savepoint.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.set_time_zone_sql
documentation:
Return the SQL that will set the connection's time zone.

Return '' if the backend doesn't support time zones.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.sequence_reset_by_name_sql
documentation:
Return a list of the SQL statements required to reset sequences
passed in `sequences`.

The `style` argument is a Style object as returned by either
color_style() or no_style() in plain.internal.legacy.management.color.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.sequence_reset_sql
documentation:
Return a list of the SQL statements required to reset sequences for
the given models.

The `style` argument is a Style object as returned by either
color_style() or no_style() in plain.internal.legacy.management.color.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.start_transaction_sql
documentation: Return the SQL statement required to start a transaction.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.end_transaction_sql
documentation: Return the SQL statement required to end a transaction.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.tablespace_sql
documentation:
Return the SQL that will be used in a query to define the tablespace.

Return '' if the backend doesn't support tablespaces.

If `inline` is True, append the SQL to a row; otherwise append it to
the entire CREATE TABLE or CREATE INDEX statement.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.prep_for_like_query
documentation: Prepare a value for use in a LIKE query.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.validate_autopk_value
documentation:
Certain backends do not accept some values for "serial" fields
(for example zero in MySQL). Raise a ValueError if the value is
invalid, otherwise return the validated value.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.adapt_unknown_value
documentation:
Transform a value to something compatible with the backend driver.

This method only depends on the type of the value. It's designed for
cases where the target type isn't known, such as .raw() SQL queries.
As a consequence it may not work perfectly in all circumstances.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.adapt_datefield_value
documentation:
Transform a date value to an object compatible with what is expected
by the backend driver for date columns.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.adapt_datetimefield_value
documentation:
Transform a datetime value to an object compatible with what is expected
by the backend driver for datetime columns.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.adapt_timefield_value
documentation:
Transform a time value to an object compatible with what is expected
by the backend driver for time columns.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.adapt_decimalfield_value
documentation:
Transform a decimal.Decimal value to an object compatible with what is
expected by the backend driver for decimal (numeric) columns.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.adapt_ipaddressfield_value
documentation:
Transform a string representation of an IP address into the expected
type for the backend driver.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.year_lookup_bounds_for_date_field
documentation:
Return a two-elements list with the lower and upper bound to be used
with a BETWEEN operator to query a DateField value using a year
lookup.

`value` is an int, containing the looked-up year.
If `iso_year` is True, return bounds for ISO-8601 week-numbering years.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.year_lookup_bounds_for_datetime_field
documentation:
Return a two-elements list with the lower and upper bound to be used
with a BETWEEN operator to query a DateTimeField value using a year
lookup.

`value` is an int, containing the looked-up year.
If `iso_year` is True, return bounds for ISO-8601 week-numbering years.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.get_db_converters
documentation:
Return a list of functions needed to convert field data.

Some field types on some backends do not provide data in the correct
format, this is the hook for converter functions.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.check_expression_support
documentation:
Check that the backend supports the provided expression.

This is used on specific backends to rule out known expressions
that have problematic or nonexistent implementations. If the
expression has a known problem, the backend should raise
NotSupportedError.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.conditional_expression_supported_in_where_clause
documentation:
Return True, if the conditional expression is supported in the WHERE
clause.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.combine_expression
documentation:
Combine a list of subexpressions into a single expression, using
the provided connecting operator. This is required because operators
can vary between backends (e.g., Oracle with %% and &) and between
subexpression types (e.g., date expressions).

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.binary_placeholder_sql
documentation:
Some backends require special syntax to insert binary content (MySQL
for example uses '_binary %s').

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.modify_insert_params
documentation:
Allow modification of insert parameters. Needed for Oracle Spatial
backend due to #10888.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.integer_field_range
documentation:
Given an integer field internal type (e.g. 'PositiveIntegerField'),
return a tuple of the (min_value, max_value) form representing the
range of the column type bound to the field.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.operations.BaseDatabaseOperations.window_frame_rows_start_end
documentation: Return SQL for start and end points in an OVER clause window frame.

Encapsulate backend-specific differences, such as the way a backend
performs ordering or calculates the ID of a recently-inserted row.

modules.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3._functions
documentation: Implementations of SQL functions for SQLite.

functions.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3._functions._sqlite_format_dtdelta
documentation:
LHS and RHS can be either:
- An integer number of microseconds
- A string representing a datetime
- A scalar value, e.g. float

modules.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.base
documentation: SQLite backend for the sqlite3 module in the standard library.

functions.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.base.decoder
documentation: Convert bytestrings from Python's sqlite3 interface to a regular string.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.base.DatabaseWrapper.check_constraints
documentation:
Check each table name in `table_names` for rows with invalid foreign
key references. This method is intended to be used in conjunction with
`disable_constraint_checking()` and `enable_constraint_checking()`, to
determine if rows with invalid references were entered while constraint
checks were off.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.base.DatabaseWrapper._start_transaction_under_autocommit
documentation:
Start a transaction explicitly in autocommit mode.

Staying in autocommit mode works around a bug of sqlite3 that breaks
savepoints when autocommit is disabled.

classes.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.base.SQLiteCursorWrapper
documentation:
Plain uses the "format" and "pyformat" styles, but Python's sqlite3 module
supports neither of these styles.

This wrapper performs the following conversions:

- "format" style to "qmark" style
- "pyformat" style to "named" style

In both cases, if you want to use a literal "%s", you'll need to use "%%s".

functions.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.introspection.get_field_size
documentation: Extract the size number from a "varchar(11)" type name

classes.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.introspection.FlexibleFieldLookupDict
documentation: This light wrapper "fakes" a dictionary interface, because some SQLite data
types include variables in them -- e.g. "varchar(30)" -- and can't be matched
as a simple dictionary lookup.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.introspection.DatabaseIntrospection.get_table_list
documentation: Return a list of table and view names in the current database.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.introspection.DatabaseIntrospection.get_table_description
documentation:
Return a description of the table with the DB-API cursor.description
interface.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.introspection.DatabaseIntrospection.get_relations
documentation:
Return a dictionary of {column_name: (ref_column_name, ref_table_name)}
representing all foreign keys in the given table.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.introspection.DatabaseIntrospection.get_constraints
documentation:
Retrieve any constraints or keys (unique, pk, fk, check, index) across
one or more columns.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.creation.DatabaseCreation.test_db_signature
documentation:
Return a tuple that uniquely identifies a test database.

This takes into account the special cases of ":memory:" and "" for
SQLite since the databases will be distinct despite having the same
TEST NAME. See https://www.sqlite.org/inmemorydb.html

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.schema.DatabaseSchemaEditor._is_referenced_by_fk_constraint
documentation:
Return whether or not the provided table name is referenced by another
one. If `column_name` is specified, only references pointing to that
column are considered. If `ignore_self` is True, self-referential
constraints are ignored.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.schema.DatabaseSchemaEditor._remake_table
documentation:
Shortcut to transform a model from old_model into new_model

This follows the correct procedure to perform non-rename or column
addition operations based on SQLite's documentation

https://www.sqlite.org/lang_altertable.html#caution

The essential steps are:
  1. Create a table with the updated definition called "new__app_model"
  2. Copy the data from the existing "app_model" table to the new table
  3. Drop the "app_model" table
  4. Rename the "new__app_model" table to "app_model"
  5. Restore any index of the previous "app_model" table.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.schema.DatabaseSchemaEditor.add_field
documentation: Create a field on a model.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.schema.DatabaseSchemaEditor.remove_field
documentation:
Remove a field from a model. Usually involves deleting a column,
but for M2Ms may involve deleting a table.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.schema.DatabaseSchemaEditor._alter_field
documentation: Perform a "physical" (non-ManyToMany) field update.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.schema.DatabaseSchemaEditor._alter_many_to_many
documentation: Alter M2Ms to repoint their to= endpoints.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.mysql.validation.DatabaseValidation.check_field_type
documentation:
MySQL has the following field length restriction:
No character (varchar) fields can have a length exceeding 255
characters if they have a unique index on them.
MySQL doesn't support a database index on some data types.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.operations.DatabaseOperations.bulk_batch_size
documentation:
SQLite has a compile-time default (SQLITE_LIMIT_VARIABLE_NUMBER) of
999 variables per query.

If there's only a single field to insert, the limit is 500
(SQLITE_MAX_COMPOUND_SELECT).

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.operations.DatabaseOperations.date_extract_sql
documentation:
Support EXTRACT with a user-defined function plain_date_extract()
that's registered in connect(). Use single quotes because this is a
string and could otherwise cause a collision with a field name.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.operations.DatabaseOperations.fetch_returned_insert_rows
documentation:
Given a cursor object that has just performed an INSERT...RETURNING
statement into a table, return the list of returned data.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.operations.DatabaseOperations.format_for_duration_arithmetic
documentation: Do nothing since formatting is handled in the custom function.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.sqlite3.operations.DatabaseOperations._quote_params_for_last_executed_query
documentation: Only for last_executed_query! Don't use this to execute SQL queries!

functions.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema._is_relevant_relation
documentation:
When altering the given field, must constraints on its model from the given
relation be temporarily dropped?

classes.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.__enter__
documentation: State-managing methods

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.execute
documentation: Execute the given SQL statement, with optional parameters.
Core utility functions

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.table_sql
documentation: Take a model and return its table definition.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._iter_column_sql
documentation: Field <-> database mapping functions

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.column_sql
documentation:
Return the column definition for a field. The field must already have
had set_attributes_from_name() called.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.skip_default
documentation:
Some backends don't accept default values for certain columns types
(i.e. MySQL longtext and longblob).

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.skip_default_on_alter
documentation:
Some backends don't accept default values for certain columns types
(i.e. MySQL longtext and longblob) in the ALTER COLUMN statement.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.prepare_default
documentation: Only used for backends which have requires_literal_defaults feature

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._column_default_sql
documentation:
Return the SQL to use in a DEFAULT clause. The resulting string should
contain a '%s' placeholder for a default value.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.effective_default
documentation: Return a field's effective database default value.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.quote_value
documentation:
Return a quoted version of the value so it's safe to use in an SQL
string. This is not safe against injection from user code; it is
intended only for use in making SQL scripts or preparing default values
for particularly tricky backends (defaults are not user-defined, though,
so this is safe).

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.create_model
documentation:
Create a table and any accompanying indexes or unique constraints for
the given `model`.
Actions

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.delete_model
documentation: Delete a model from the database.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.add_index
documentation: Add an index on a model.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.remove_index
documentation: Remove an index from a model.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.add_constraint
documentation: Add a constraint to a model.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.remove_constraint
documentation: Remove a constraint from a model.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.alter_db_table
documentation: Rename the table a model points to.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.alter_db_tablespace
documentation: Move a model's table between tablespaces.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.add_field
documentation:
Create a field on a model. Usually involves adding a column, but may
involve adding a table instead (for M2M fields).

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.remove_field
documentation:
Remove a field from a model. Usually involves deleting a column,
but for M2Ms may involve deleting a table.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.alter_field
documentation:
Allow a field's type, uniqueness, nullability, default, column,
constraints, etc. to be modified.
`old_field` is required to compute the necessary changes.
If `strict` is True, raise errors if the old column does not match
`old_field` precisely.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._alter_field
documentation: Perform a "physical" (non-ManyToMany) field update.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._alter_column_null_sql
documentation:
Hook to specialize column null alteration.

Return a (sql, params) fragment to set a column to null or non-null
as required by new_field, or None if no changes are required.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._alter_column_default_sql
documentation:
Hook to specialize column default alteration.

Return a (sql, params) fragment to add or drop (depending on the drop
argument) a default to new_field's column.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._alter_column_type_sql
documentation:
Hook to specialize column type alteration for different backends,
for cases when a creation type is different to an alteration type
(e.g. SERIAL in PostgreSQL, PostGIS fields).

Return a two-tuple of: an SQL fragment of (sql, params) to insert into
an ALTER TABLE statement and a list of extra (sql, params) tuples to
run once the field is altered.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._alter_many_to_many
documentation: Alter M2Ms to repoint their to= endpoints.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._create_index_name
documentation:
Generate a unique name for an index/unique constraint.

The name is divided into 3 parts: the table name, the column names,
and a unique digest and suffix.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._create_index_sql
documentation:
Return the SQL statement to create the index for one or several fields
or expressions. `sql` can be specified if the syntax differs from the
standard (GIS indexes, ...).

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._model_indexes_sql
documentation: Return a list of all index SQL statements (field indexes, Meta.indexes) for the specified model.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._field_indexes_sql
documentation: Return a list of all index SQL statements for the specified field.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.base.schema.BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._constraint_names
documentation: Return all constraint names matching the columns and conditions.

This class and its subclasses are responsible for emitting schema-changing
statements to the databases - model creation/removal/alteration, field
renaming, index fiddling, and so on.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.mysql.introspection.DatabaseIntrospection.get_table_list
documentation: Return a list of table and view names in the current database.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.mysql.introspection.DatabaseIntrospection.get_table_description
documentation:
Return a description of the table with the DB-API cursor.description
interface."

methods.name: plain.models.backends.mysql.introspection.DatabaseIntrospection.get_relations
documentation:
Return a dictionary of {field_name: (field_name_other_table, other_table)}
representing all foreign keys in the given table.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.mysql.introspection.DatabaseIntrospection.get_storage_engine
documentation:
Retrieve the storage engine for a given table. Return the default
storage engine if the table doesn't exist.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.mysql.introspection.DatabaseIntrospection.get_constraints
documentation:
Retrieve any constraints or keys (unique, pk, fk, check, index) across
one or more columns.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.mysql.schema.DatabaseSchemaEditor._create_missing_fk_index
documentation:
MySQL can remove an implicit FK index on a field when that field is
covered by another index. "covered" here means
that the more complex index has the FK field as its first field (see
https://bugs.mysql.com/bug.php?id=37910).

Manually create an implicit FK index to make it possible to remove the
composed index.

methods.name: plain.models.backends.mysql.schema.DatabaseSchemaEditor._set_field_new_type_null_status
documentation:

They also act as reverse fields for the purposes of the Meta API because
they're the closest concept currently available.

argument is another model, or a reference to another model - see
`resolve_relation()` for the various forms these may take. Any relative
references will be resolved relative to `model`.

This is a convenience wrapper for `Packages.lazy_model_operation` - the app
registry model used is the one found in `model._meta.packages`.

modules.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors
documentation:
Accessors for related objects.

When a field defines a relation between two models, each model class provides
an attribute to access related instances of the other model class (unless the
reverse accessor has been disabled with related_name='+').

Accessors are implemented as descriptors in order to customize access and
assignment. This module defines the descriptor classes.

Forward accessors follow foreign keys. Reverse accessors trace them back. For
example, with the following models:

```
   class Parent(Model):
       pass

   class Child(Model):
       parent = ForeignKey(Parent, related_name='children')

``child.parent`` is a forward many-to-one relation. ``parent.children`` is a
```

reverse many-to-one relation.

There are three types of relations (many-to-one, one-to-one, and many-to-many)
and two directions (forward and reverse) for a total of six combinations.

1. Related instance on the forward side of a many-to-one relation:
   `ForwardManyToOneDescriptor`.

   Uniqueness of foreign key values is irrelevant to accessing the related
   instance, making the many-to-one and one-to-one cases identical as far as
   the descriptor is concerned. The constraint is checked upstream (unicity
   validation in forms) or downstream (unique indexes in the database).

2. Related instance on the forward side of a one-to-one
   relation: `ForwardOneToOneDescriptor`.

   It avoids querying the database when accessing the parent link field in
   a multi-table inheritance scenario.

3. Related instance on the reverse side of a one-to-one relation:
   `ReverseOneToOneDescriptor`.

   One-to-one relations are asymmetrical, despite the apparent symmetry of the
   name, because they're implemented in the database with a foreign key from
   one table to another. As a consequence `ReverseOneToOneDescriptor` is
   slightly different from `ForwardManyToOneDescriptor`.

4. Related objects manager for related instances on the reverse side of a
   many-to-one relation: `ReverseManyToOneDescriptor`.

   Unlike the previous two classes, this one provides access to a collection
   of objects. It returns a manager rather than an instance.

5. Related objects manager for related instances on the forward or reverse
   sides of a many-to-many relation: `ManyToManyDescriptor`.

   Many-to-many relations are symmetrical. The syntax of Plain models
   requires declaring them on one side but that's an implementation detail.
   They could be declared on the other side without any change in behavior.
   Therefore the forward and reverse descriptors can be the same.

   If you're looking for `ForwardManyToManyDescriptor` or
   `ReverseManyToManyDescriptor`, use `ManyToManyDescriptor` instead.

documentation:
Create a manager for the reverse side of a many-to-one relation.

This manager subclasses another manager, generally the default manager of
the related model, and adds behaviors specific to many-to-one relations.

functions.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.create_forward_many_to_many_manager
documentation:
Create a manager for the either side of a many-to-many relation.

This manager subclasses another manager, generally the default manager of
the related model, and adds behaviors specific to many-to-many relations.

classes.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.ForwardManyToOneDescriptor

methods.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.ForwardManyToOneDescriptor.__get__
documentation:
Get the related instance through the forward relation.

With the example above, when getting `child.parent`:

- `self` is the descriptor managing the `parent` attribute
- `instance` is the `child` instance
- `cls` is the `Child` class (we don't need it)

methods.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.ForwardManyToOneDescriptor.__set__
documentation:
Set the related instance through the forward relation.

With the example above, when setting `child.parent = parent`:

- `self` is the descriptor managing the `parent` attribute
- `instance` is the `child` instance
- `value` is the `parent` instance on the right of the equal sign

methods.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.ForwardManyToOneDescriptor.__reduce__
documentation:
Pickling should return the instance attached by self.field on the
model, not a new copy of that descriptor. Use getattr() to retrieve
the instance directly from the model.

Accessor to the related object on the forward side of a many-to-one or
one-to-one (via ForwardOneToOneDescriptor subclass) relation.

In the example:

```
class Child(Model):
    parent = ForeignKey(Parent, related_name='children')
```

`Child.parent` is a `ForwardManyToOneDescriptor` instance.

classes.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.ForwardOneToOneDescriptor
documentation:
Accessor to the related object on the forward side of a one-to-one relation.

In the example:

```
class Restaurant(Model):
    place = OneToOneField(Place, related_name='restaurant')
```

`Restaurant.place` is a `ForwardOneToOneDescriptor` instance.

classes.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.ReverseOneToOneDescriptor

methods.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.ReverseOneToOneDescriptor.__get__
documentation:
Get the related instance through the reverse relation.

With the example above, when getting `place.restaurant`:

- `self` is the descriptor managing the `restaurant` attribute
- `instance` is the `place` instance
- `cls` is the `Place` class (unused)

Keep in mind that `Restaurant` holds the foreign key to `Place`.

methods.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.ReverseOneToOneDescriptor.__set__
documentation:
Set the related instance through the reverse relation.

With the example above, when setting `place.restaurant = restaurant`:

- `self` is the descriptor managing the `restaurant` attribute
- `instance` is the `place` instance
- `value` is the `restaurant` instance on the right of the equal sign

Keep in mind that `Restaurant` holds the foreign key to `Place`.

Accessor to the related object on the reverse side of a one-to-one
relation.

In the example:

```
class Restaurant(Model):
    place = OneToOneField(Place, related_name='restaurant')
```

`Place.restaurant` is a `ReverseOneToOneDescriptor` instance.

classes.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.ReverseManyToOneDescriptor

methods.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.ReverseManyToOneDescriptor.__get__
documentation:
Get the related objects through the reverse relation.

With the example above, when getting `parent.children`:

- `self` is the descriptor managing the `children` attribute
- `instance` is the `parent` instance
- `cls` is the `Parent` class (unused)

Accessor to the related objects manager on the reverse side of a
many-to-one relation.

In the example:

```
class Child(Model):
    parent = ForeignKey(Parent, related_name='children')
```

`Parent.children` is a `ReverseManyToOneDescriptor` instance.

Most of the implementation is delegated to a dynamically defined manager
class built by `create_forward_many_to_many_manager()` defined below.

classes.name: plain.models.fields.related_descriptors.ManyToManyDescriptor
documentation:
Accessor to the related objects manager on the forward and reverse sides of
a many-to-many relation.

In the example:

```
class Pizza(Model):
    toppings = ManyToManyField(Topping, related_name='pizzas')
```

`Pizza.toppings` and `Topping.pizzas` are `ManyToManyDescriptor`
instances.

Most of the implementation is delegated to a dynamically defined manager
class built by `create_forward_many_to_many_manager()` defined below.

methods.name: plain.models.fields.Field.deconstruct
documentation:
Return enough information to recreate the field as a 4-tuple:

 * The name of the field on the model, if contribute_to_class() has
   been run.
 * The import path of the field, including the class, e.g.
   plain.models.IntegerField. This should be the most portable
   version, so less specific may be better.
 * A list of positional arguments.
 * A dict of keyword arguments.

Note that the positional or keyword arguments must contain values of
the following types (including inner values of collection types):

 * None, bool, str, int, float, complex, set, frozenset, list, tuple,
   dict
 * UUID
 * datetime.datetime (naive), datetime.date
 * top-level classes, top-level functions - will be referenced by their
   full import path
 * Storage instances - these have their own deconstruct() method

This is because the values here must be serialized into a text format
(possibly new Python code, possibly JSON) and these are the only types
with encoding handlers defined.

There's no need to return the exact way the field was instantiated this
time, just ensure that the resulting field is the same - prefer keyword
arguments over positional ones, and omit parameters with their default
values.

classes.name: plain.models.fields.AutoFieldMeta
documentation:
Metaclass to maintain backward inheritance compatibility for AutoField.

It is intended that AutoFieldMixin become public API when it is possible to
create a non-integer automatically-generated field using column defaults
stored in the database.

In many areas Plain also relies on using isinstance() to check for an
automatically-generated field as a subclass of AutoField. A new flag needs
to be implemented on Field to be used instead.

When these issues have been addressed, this metaclass could be used to
deprecate inheritance from AutoField and use of isinstance() with AutoField
for detecting automatically-generated fields.

functions.name: plain.models.transaction.get_rollback
documentation: Get the "needs rollback" flag -- for *advanced use* only.

functions.name: plain.models.transaction.set_rollback
documentation:
Set or unset the "needs rollback" flag -- for *advanced use* only.

When `rollback` is `True`, trigger a rollback when exiting the innermost
enclosing atomic block that has `savepoint=True` (that's the default). Use
this to force a rollback without raising an exception.

When `rollback` is `False`, prevent such a rollback. Use this only after
rolling back to a known-good state! Otherwise, you break the atomic block
and data corruption may occur.

functions.name: plain.models.transaction.mark_for_rollback_on_error
documentation:
Internal low-level utility to mark a transaction as "needs rollback" when
an exception is raised while not enforcing the enclosed block to be in a
transaction. This is needed by Model.save() and friends to avoid starting a
transaction when in autocommit mode and a single query is executed.

It's equivalent to:

    connection = get_connection(using)
    if connection.get_autocommit():
        yield
    else:
        with transaction.atomic(using=using, savepoint=False):
            yield

but it uses low-level utilities to avoid performance overhead.

classes.name: plain.models.transaction.Atomic
documentation:
Guarantee the atomic execution of a given block.

An instance can be used either as a decorator or as a context manager.

When it's used as a decorator, __call__ wraps the execution of the
decorated function in the instance itself, used as a context manager.

When it's used as a context manager, __enter__ creates a transaction or a
savepoint, depending on whether a transaction is already in progress, and
__exit__ commits the transaction or releases the savepoint on normal exit,
and rolls back the transaction or to the savepoint on exceptions.

It's possible to disable the creation of savepoints if the goal is to
ensure that some code runs within a transaction without creating overhead.

A stack of savepoints identifiers is maintained as an attribute of the
connection. None denotes the absence of a savepoint.

This allows reentrancy even if the same AtomicWrapper is reused. For
example, it's possible to define `oa = atomic('other')` and use `@oa` or
`with oa:` multiple times.

Since database connections are thread-local, this is thread-safe.

An atomic block can be tagged as durable. In this case, raise a
RuntimeError if it's nested within another atomic block. This guarantees
that database changes in a durable block are committed to the database when
the block exists without error.

This is a private API.
Decorators / context managers #

methods.name: plain.models.options.Options._get_fields
documentation:
Internal helper function to return fields of the model.
* If forward=True, then fields defined on this model are returned.
* If reverse=True, then relations pointing to this model are returned.
* If include_hidden=True, then fields with is_hidden=True are returned.
* The include_parents argument toggles if fields from parent models
  should be included. It has three values: True, False, and
  PROXY_PARENTS. When set to PROXY_PARENTS, the call will return all
  fields defined for the current model or any of its parents in the
  parent chain to the model's concrete model.

methods.name: plain.models.expressions.BaseExpression.as_sql
documentation:
Responsible for returning a (sql, [params]) tuple to be included
in the current query.

Different backends can provide their own implementation, by
providing an `as_{vendor}` method and patching the Expression:

```
def override_as_sql(self, compiler, connection):
    # custom logic
    return super().as_sql(compiler, connection)
setattr(Expression, 'as_' + connection.vendor, override_as_sql)
```

Arguments:
 * compiler: the query compiler responsible for generating the query.
   Must have a compile method, returning a (sql, [params]) tuple.
   Calling compiler(value) will return a quoted `value`.

 * connection: the database connection used for the current query.

Return: (sql, params)
  Where `sql` is a string containing ordered sql parameters to be
  replaced with the elements of the list `params`.

methods.name: plain.models.expressions.BaseExpression.resolve_expression
documentation:
Provide the chance to do any preprocessing or validation before being
added to the query.

Arguments:
 * query: the backend query implementation
 * allow_joins: boolean allowing or denying use of joins
   in this query
 * reuse: a set of reusable joins for multijoins
 * summarize: a terminal aggregate clause
 * for_save: whether this expression about to be used in a save or update

Return: an Expression to be added to the query.

methods.name: plain.models.expressions.BaseExpression.convert_value
documentation:
Expressions provide their own converters because users have the option
of manually specifying the output_field which may be a different type
from the one the database returns.

methods.name: plain.models.expressions.BaseExpression.get_source_fields
documentation: Return the underlying field types used by this aggregate.

methods.name: plain.models.expressions.BaseExpression.flatten
documentation:
Recursively yield this expression and all subexpressions, in
depth-first order.

methods.name: plain.models.expressions.BaseExpression.select_format
documentation:
Custom format for select clauses. For example, EXISTS expressions need
to be wrapped in CASE WHEN on Oracle.
Base class for all query expressions.

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.Expression
documentation: An expression that can be combined with other expressions.

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.F

methods.name: plain.models.expressions.F.__init__
documentation:
Arguments:
 * name: the name of the field this expression references
An object capable of resolving references to existing query objects.

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.ResolvedOuterRef
documentation:
An object that contains a reference to an outer query.

In this case, the reference to the outer query has been resolved because
the inner query has been used as a subquery.

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.Func

methods.name: plain.models.expressions.Func._get_repr_options
documentation: Return a dict of extra __init__() options to include in the repr.

class_variables.name: plain.models.expressions.Func.arity
documentation: The number of arguments the function accepts.
An SQL function call.

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.Value

methods.name: plain.models.expressions.Value.__init__
documentation:
Arguments:
 * value: the value this expression represents. The value will be
   added into the sql parameter list and properly quoted.

 * output_field: an instance of the model field type that this
   expression will return, such as IntegerField() or CharField().
Represent a wrapped value as a node within an expression.

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.Ref
documentation:
Reference to column alias of the query. For example, Ref('sum_cost') in
qs.annotate(sum_cost=Sum('cost')) query.

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.ExpressionList
documentation:
An expression containing multiple expressions. Can be used to provide a
list of expressions as an argument to another expression, like a partition
clause.

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.ExpressionWrapper
documentation:
An expression that can wrap another expression so that it can provide
extra context to the inner expression, such as the output_field.

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.NegatedExpression
documentation: The logical negation of a conditional expression.

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.Case
documentation:
An SQL searched CASE expression:

    CASE
        WHEN n > 0
            THEN 'positive'
        WHEN n < 0
            THEN 'negative'
        ELSE 'zero'
    END

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.Subquery
documentation:
An explicit subquery. It may contain OuterRef() references to the outer
query which will be resolved when it is applied to that query.

classes.name: plain.models.expressions.WindowFrame
documentation:
Model the frame clause in window expressions. There are two types of frame
clauses which are subclasses, however, all processing and validation (by no
means intended to be complete) is done here. Thus, providing an end for a
frame is optional (the default is UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING, which is the last
row in the frame).
